response.justification: Sarah Smith has a broad and genuinely impressive full-stack career, but she is a poor match for this role. The single most disqualifying gap is C++: the role requires 8 years of C++ experience, yet her resume shows only a 6-month stint at National Instruments writing C/C++ components and a bachelor thesis project — C++ does not even appear in her listed skills. Her primary languages are TypeScript, Node.js, and DLang. Beyond the C++ gap, her cloud experience is incidental (AWS CDK for a pricing service, personal Kubernetes deployment) rather than the production-scale cloud compute platform engineering the role demands. Her leadership is largely informal — Staff Engineer titles and community workshops — with no evidence of formal technical leadership over cloud or infrastructure engineering teams.
strengths: Demonstrated exposure to high-throughput distributed systems (millions of HTTP requests/second at Dunnhumby)
Strong mentoring and knowledge-sharing track record through workshops, conference talks, and community courses
Broad infrastructure exposure including Kubernetes, Docker, CI/CD pipelines, and AWS CDK
concerns: C++ experience is essentially absent — roughly 6 months at National Instruments versus the 8-year minimum required
No enterprise-scale cloud compute platform engineering; cloud usage is incidental and shallow relative to role expectations
No formal technical leadership of cloud or infrastructure engineering teams; staff titles reflect seniority, not people/project leadership accountability
